birth.historical_notes: DATE DISCREPANCY: Findagrave records 3 Dec 1868. FamilySearch records 31 Dec 1868. Going with Findagrave (cemetery primary source). Father Charles Gay Hooker (H02703) was 48; mother Maria Celia Osgood (X02249) was 32. State: California.

marriages.notes: George Heydenfeldt Solomon Lent (1868-1942) — same year birth/death as Bessie. Business partner with Bessie's brother Robert Gay Hooker (H04266) in Hooker & Lent / Lent & Hooker real estate (14 Post Street SF + Mills Building). Bessie's nephew Rodman Lent Hooker (HD3246, Sam Talcott Hooker's grandfather) was named for the Lent partnership.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into her brother's business partner
narrative_blocks.body: Bessie Augusta Hooker — daughter of Charles Gay Hooker and Maria Celia Osgood — married George Heydenfeldt Solomon Lent on October 8, 1892 in San Francisco. George (1868-1942) was the business partner of Bessie's brother Robert Gay Hooker in the firm Hooker & Lent (later Lent & Hooker), real estate, with offices at 14 Post Street SF and the Mills Building. The marriage drew the two families' commercial fortunes still closer.

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker line continues to modern Howard cousins
narrative_blocks.body: Through Bessie Augusta and George H. Lent the Hooker line continues into a separate branch of modern California cousins. Their only child Elizabeth Lent (, b.1914) married Dr. Lot D.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote condolence letter to grieving Jane Stanford
narrative_blocks.body: In 1884 — when Bessie Augusta was 16 — Leland Stanford Jr. died of typhoid fever in Florence at age 15, the only son of California's railroad king and former governor Leland Stanford Sr. and his wife Jane. Bessie Augusta Hooker (later Mrs. George H. Lent) wrote a personal condolence letter to Jane Stanford, signing 'Bessie Hooker Lent' from her San Francisco address at 2229 Washington Street: 'Words at such a time, I know, seem cold and meaningless and bring but little comfort, but I felt I could but tell you, after your great kindness to me, how much we sympathize and thought of you in your great sorrow.' The letter is preserved in the Stanford University Archives among the condolence letters re: the death of Leland Stanford Jr. The Hookers and Stanfords moved in overlapping San Francisco mercantile circles; Leland Jr.'s memory two years later (1886) became the foundation of Stanford University.

narrative_blocks.header: Corresponded with botanist Alice Eastwood, 1926
narrative_blocks.body: In 1926 Bessie Hooker Lent appears in the California Academy of Sciences Archives as a correspondent of Alice Eastwood — the legendary botanist and Director of Botany at the Academy from 1894-1949 (with her famous 1906 rescue of 1,500 type specimens during the San Francisco earthquake fire). The single dated entry from that year places Bessie among Eastwood's network of botanical correspondents alongside the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew, the Imperial Botanical Garden in St. Petersburg, and Huntington Gardens. Bessie's interest in botany echoes the Hooker family's long scientific tradition — though no relation, the family shared the surname with Sir William Jackson Hooker (founding Director of Kew) and Sir Joseph Dalton Hooker.

cross_connections.evidence: Bessie's husband George H. Lent partnered with her brother Robert Gay Hooker (H04266) in Hooker & Lent real estate, San Francisco.

bio.extra_names: FamilySearch records spelling variant 'Haydenfelt' (without 'e'); Lent family book + Wikipedia spelling 'Heydenfeldt' is canonical via the Solomon Heydenfeldt namesake.

birth.historical_notes: Per Lent family book entry #78: b. January 23, 1868. Likely San Francisco given his father William M. Lent's SF residence.

death.note: Cremated at Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma per Findagrave + cemetery records.

sources.short: James Arthur Lent, History of the Lent (van Lent) Family in America (1902)
FamilySearch: George Haydenfelt Lent / Bessie Augusta Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Hooker & Lent SF real estate firm
narrative_blocks.body: His 'Hooker & Lent' San Francisco real estate firm at 503 Market Street operated from the 1890s through at least 1932. Partners were his brother-in-law Robert G Hooker (Bessie's brother) and his own brother Eugene Lent — a family business joining the Lent and Hooker SF dynasties.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hooker men already Bohemian Club fellows
narrative_blocks.body: He joined the Bohemian Club in 1892 — alongside Bessie's brothers G Osgood Hooker and Robert G Hooker, who were already fellows the year of his October wedding. The three Hooker family men formed the anchor of the social circle George married into.

narrative_blocks.header: Predeceased wife Bessie by five months, both 1942
narrative_blocks.body: He died 9 March 1942 in San Francisco at seventy-four. His wife Bessie Augusta Hooker died five months later on 19 August 1942 — both cremated at Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma.

narrative_blocks.header: Likely named for Justice Solomon Heydenfeldt
narrative_blocks.body: His middle name almost certainly honors Solomon Heydenfeldt (1816-1890), Charleston-born Jewish jurist who served as California Supreme Court Justice 1852-1857. Heydenfeldt was prominent in San Francisco where George's father William M. Lent lived.

cross_connections.display_label: Married Robert's sister Bessie; partners in Hooker & Lent firm.
Bohemian Club fellows the year he married Hooker's sister.
Robert Gay's son carries 'Lent' as his middle name.
Robert Gay's grandson carries 'Lent' as his middle name.

careers.notes: Per his father William Mandeville Lent's October 1904 SF Call obituary: 'George H. Lent of the real estate firm of Hooker & Lent.' The firm was a major SF real estate and investment business at 503 Market Street. Partners with Robert G. Hooker (H04266) — Bessie's brother — and brother Eugene Lent (X-prefix above). The firm operated through at least 1932 per the SF Real Estate Board Yearbook.

X02298.siblings_research: Eldest known siblings (per Lent book #48): William H. Lent (#75, b. Mar 16, 1858 — d. without issue), Fanny A. Lent (#76, b. Mar 18, 1862 — never married), Eugene Lent (#77, b. Sep 12, 1863 — X-prefix above)

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma San Mateo Co CA. Cremated.

memberships.notes: Per Bohemian Club Officers & Members 1892 roster: 'LENT, George H.' Listed alongside brothers-in-law G. Osgood Hooker and Robert G. Hooker — three Hooker family members in the Bohemian Club the same year George married Bessie.

bio.bio_blurb: Only child bridging the Hooker-Lent and Howard lines

birth.historical_notes: Per Sam family knowledge: only child of Bessie Augusta Hooker (H04268) and George H. Lent. Born 1914 — likely SF given parents' SF residence.

death.note: Deceased per Tony Hooker pedigree ("1914-Deceased"); specific year not yet documented in available sources. Predeceased her son Lent Howard (d.2023) per his obituary which lists her as "predeceased by his mother."

marriages.notes: At least 4 children: TWIN SONS Duncan Lent Howard (HD3250, b.1940 LIVING — previously misreported as deceased 2012, that was wrong) and George Lent Howard (HD3251, 1940-2012); plus daughters Pamela Howard (m. Castle) and Tracey Howard Pullen (m. Pullen) — both daughters intentionally not built (status uncertain).

narrative_blocks.header: Bridge from Hooker line to modern Howard cousins
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Lent (b.1914) was the only child of Bessie Augusta Hooker and George Heydenfeldt Solomon Lent (her brother-in-law to Robert Gay Hooker through the Hooker-Lent real estate partnership). She married Dr. Lot D. Howard Jr., son of Lot Duncan Howard Sr. — a Stanford-trained zoologist who authored two anatomy textbooks at Stanford University Press in 1928-29 with Edwin Chapin Starks: 'The Dissection of the Turtle' (1928) and 'The Dissection of the Toad' (1929).

narrative_blocks.header: Twin sons born the same September day
narrative_blocks.body: Bessie Hooker’s only child married Dr. Lot Howard Jr. and bore twin sons in San Francisco on September 9, 1940 — Duncan and George Lent Howard — plus daughters Pamela and Tracey, carrying the Hooker-Lent line into the modern Howard cousins.

parents.father_research_notes: Lot Duncan Howard Sr. — Stanford-trained zoologist; co-authored "The Dissection of the Turtle" (1928) and "The Dissection of the Toad" (1929) at Stanford University Press with Edwin Chapin Starks. Texts available via HathiTrust.

marriages.notes: At least 4 children: Duncan Lent Howard (HD3250, LIVING b.1940), George Lent Howard (HD3251, 1940-2012), and daughters Pamela Howard Castle + Tracey Howard Pullen (not built).

education.institution: California Polytechnic State University (Cal Poly San Luis Obispo)
education.notes: Animal Science / Animal Husbandry (per his own listing). Same Cal Poly Animal Husbandry program his twin brother George Lent Howard attended briefly — the cowboy interest was a shared family thread.
Per his own social media / education listing.

narrative_blocks.header: Twin born to Hooker-line Elizabeth Lent
narrative_blocks.body: Duncan Lent Howard was born September 9, 1940 in San Francisco, the elder twin of Lot D. Howard Jr. and Elizabeth Lent — making him the great-grandson of Bessie Augusta Hooker and through her a fifth-generation San Franciscan. His twin brother George Lent Howard shared the same birthday but lived a more rural-Western California cowboy life, while Duncan stayed engaged with San Francisco professional and academic worlds.

narrative_blocks.header: Animal husbandry, pre-law, then USF history
narrative_blocks.body: Duncan attended Cal Poly San Luis Obispo 1959-1961 studying Animal Science / Husbandry — the same program his twin George briefly attended for cowboy-life training. He later did pre-law at City College of San Francisco, then completed his Bachelor of Arts in Government and History at the University of San Francisco in 1970. The pivot from rural agriculture to humanities and pre-law tracked the tension between the two twins: George went west toward livestock and earth-moving, Duncan stayed urban.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Madeline Domning, raised two San Francisco sons
narrative_blocks.body: Duncan married Madeline Domning Howard (, b.Jun 18, 1942 Lima Peru — daughter of Loyal Gordon Domning 1913-2001 and Mozelle Alberta Lundy 1914-2006). Madeline died in San Francisco October 25, 1983 at age 41. Their two sons — Lent Duncan Howard (, 1968-2023) and Lyman Domning Howard (, b.1969) — both attended UC Berkeley.

death.cause: Following a prolonged convalescence resulting from a major stroke in 2007. Died at Western Slope Care Center, Placerville CA.

burial.plot_notes: Remains laid to rest on a hillside near Hana, Maui HI — a place he loved for its beauty, traditions, and the kindness of the native people.

marriages.notes: First marriage to Diane Nickerson. Two sons George L. Howard Jr. and Timothy L. Howard.
Second wife Pamela. Pamela had three children from a previous relationship: Ellen, Blaine, and Alexandra (George's stepchildren).

sources.short: George Lent Howard obituary (Marin Independent Journal, Nov 2012)
Sam Talcott Hooker family knowledge (cousin confirmation)

education.notes: Brief attendance at this college-prep ranch school where George 'fell in love with the cowboy way of life.'

education.institution: California Polytechnic State University (Cal Poly San Luis Obispo)
education.notes: Brief undergraduate work in Animal Husbandry / Livestock Management.

careers.employer: His own livestock transportation companies + earth-moving businesses
careers.location: Bay Area / Marin County / Sierra foothills
careers.notes: Cattle truck operator + dump truck / bulldozer earth-moving. 'Above all else, he loved to drive ... anything.'

narrative_blocks.header: 'This Old Cowboy' — Marin County livestock hauler
narrative_blocks.body: George Lent Howard — the elder twin of Duncan Lent Howard by a few minutes — was born September 9, 1940 in San Francisco, a fourth-generation native. He attended Grant Elementary, Town School for Boys, Tamalpais High, Lowell High, and briefly the Orme School (a college-prep ranch school in north-central Arizona) where he fell in love with the cowboy way of life. He went on to study Animal Husbandry at Cal Poly San Luis Obispo.

narrative_blocks.header: Livestock truck driver and earth-mover
narrative_blocks.body: George owned several livestock transportation companies and earth-moving businesses across the Bay Area. Per his obituary, his passion in life was 'manning the wheel' — whether a cattle truck, a dump truck or bulldozer for one of his earth-moving operations, or his Lincoln Town Car on a Sunday afternoon. His humor was 'wickedly wry' and his 'heartfelt devotion to friends and family' was famous in his community.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Placerville care center after stroke
narrative_blocks.body: In 2007 George suffered a major stroke and entered prolonged convalescence at Western Slope Care Center in Placerville CA. He died there in his sleep on November 10, 2012 at age 72, surrounded by his wife Pamela, twin brother Duncan (, who survived him), sisters Pamela Howard Castle and Tracey Howard Pullen, and many nephews and grandchildren. His remains were taken to Hawaii and laid to rest on a hillside near Hana, Maui — a place near to his heart for its beauty and its respectful observance of ancient Hawaiian traditions.

Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hooker brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay arrived in San Francisco in spring 1851 and partnered with younger brother John Delos Hooker at Hooker & Co., 117–119 California Street — the hardware fortune that later funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson. Older brother Henry Clay was simultaneously building Arizona's largest cattle empire.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined the Sons of the American Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay Hooker joined the Sons of the American Revolution, formally documenting his descent from a Revolutionary-era family. He died of pneumonia at age 83 on November 19, 1905, at his home at 967 Bush Street in San Francisco. Buried at Mountain View Cemetery in Oakland — the Bay Area's premier Victorian garden cemetery. (A Findagrave geocoding error lists the death location as 'San Francisco del Mar Municipality, Oaxaca, Mexico'; the actual location is San Francisco, California.)

narrative_blocks.header: A homestead chair followed him to San Francisco
narrative_blocks.body: He took a child's side chair from the Hinsdale homestead with him to San Francisco in 1851. The chair returned east after his 1905 death and now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society in Concord, provenanced back to his great-grandfather Rev. Bunker Gay.

cross_connections.display_label: Carried his great-grandfather Bunker's Gay surname as a middle name

burial.plot_notes: Mountain View Cemetery, Oakland, Alameda County CA (per Findagrave Memorial 255641260).

careers.employer: Hooker & Co. (with brother John Delos Hooker + Isaac H. Cory)
careers.notes: Came to San Francisco in spring 1851; built fortune in hardware import. Hooker & Co. imported hardware, iron, and related goods. Partnered with brother John Delos Hooker (H02709, who later founded the Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson Observatory).

Per Truman & Hooker partner biography (re. son Osgood): 'Charles G. Hooker, a prominent hardware merchant of California, who came to this State in 1852, and still resides in San Francisco.' Charles arrived CA 1852 — placed him in the second wave of Gold Rush migrants (1849-1852). Hardware merchandise business connects to the broader CA development boom serving miners + settlers.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up on the Hinsdale homestead before sailing to California in 1851; the family chair he took with him now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society.

bio.extra_names: Tony Hooker pedigree records full first name as 'Arrabella' — formal name 'Arrabella Maria Celia Osgood'. EH 1909 lists her simply as 'Maria Celia (Osgood)' (using middle/Latin name in everyday use).

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909

Findagrave
Edward Hooker 1909

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Inherited the Hinsdale homestead from his father Seth; died in the house where he had been born ninety-three years earlier.

career.notes: Learned the trade in early life; pursued carriage making while also farming the homestead.
Worked the homestead through old age; gave full attention to farming after abandoning the carriage trade.

narrative_blocks.header: His sons launched three California empires
narrative_blocks.body: Six of his eleven children left Hinsdale for the West. Charles Gay built the San Francisco hardware fortune, Henry Clay founded Arizona's first cattle ranch, and John Daggett funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson.

narrative_blocks.header: Ninety-three years in one Hinsdale farmhouse
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 1791 in the room overlooking the Gay homestead and dying in March 1885, he passed his entire life on one farm in Hinsdale. The Gazetteer of Cheshire County called him 'a hale and hearty old man.'

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed to California twice in his eighties
narrative_blocks.body: After eighty he made two trips out to visit his California sons, riding the railroads across a continent he had been born too early to see imagined. He returned each time to the Hinsdale homestead.

parents.father_research_notes: Simeon Daggett (1758-1819). Per WikiTree (cited in Sam's research). Likely descended from the Massachusetts colonial Daggett line (John Daggett of Watertown MA, immigrant ~1630s).
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Dean (1758-c.1819). The matching birth/death dates with Simeon (per WikiTree) are likely a data error in the WikiTree record — almost certainly Anna had different death date than Simeon. Maiden name Dean.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Henry Clay Hooker, Arizona cattle baron
narrative_blocks.body: She raised eleven children at Hinsdale on the Connecticut River. Her son Henry Clay Hooker founded the Sierra Bonita Ranch in Arizona Territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Daggetts married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1816 marriage to Henry Hooker came four years after Elisabeth Daggett, in a separate Hooker branch, married Edward Hooker. The two Daggett brides may share earlier Massachusetts ancestry.

X02247.siblings_research: Per WikiTree: 8 documented Daggett siblings: Simeon Jr (M, 1780-1854), Sarah M. (F, 1783-?), (unnamed son b.1784-?), Nathaniel (M, 1785-1826), Nancy (F, 1788-1854), Caleb C (M, 1792-1879), Silvey (F, 1792-1883 — possibly twin of Caleb), and Mary herself (1795-1864). Mary was the youngest of 8.
